title: Infective Endocarditis — prophylaxis
oneLiner: Antibiotics given before certain procedures to prevent life-threatening valve infection in the highest-risk patients only — misuse is common.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
You are on call for the ward. The nurse asks you to review a 65-year-old man before his dental extraction tomorrow. He has a history of “heart murmur.” His drug chart shows amoxicillin 2 g PO stat prescribed. You check his notes: he had a mitral valve repair with an annuloplasty ring 10 years ago. Two months ago, he was treated for a dental abscess with antibiotics. You need to decide if this prophylaxis is appropriate.

**Dissecting the vignette:**

- **“Heart murmur”** — not specific; you need to know the *type* of valve lesion. Some murmurs (e.g., mitral valve prolapse without regurgitation) are NOT an indication. Others (prosthetic valve, previous IE) are.
- **“Mitral valve repair with annuloplasty ring”** — this is a prosthetic material used in repair. Guidelines consider any prosthetic valve (including annuloplasty rings) as **high-risk** for IE. Prophylaxis is recommended.
- **“Dental extraction”** — a procedure that involves manipulation of gingiva or periapical region, so it triggers prophylaxis in high-risk patients.
- **“Amoxicillin 2 g PO stat”** — correct dose and timing (30–60 minutes before procedure). But you still need to confirm the patient is truly high-risk.
- **“Treated for dental abscess two months ago”** — prior dental infection is a risk factor for IE, but not an indication for prophylaxis unless the patient is already high-risk.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **The patient with a “normal” echocardiogram** — e.g., a bicuspid aortic valve without stenosis or regurgitation is considered moderate risk in some guidelines, but current NICE and ESC do **not** recommend prophylaxis even for bicuspid valves. Many junior doctors over-prescribe here.
- **The elderly patient with a prosthetic valve having a colonoscopy** — older guidelines (pre-2008) recommended prophylaxis for GI procedures. Current guidelines say **no** for GI/GU procedures, even in high-risk. But some patients or dentists may still request it.
- **The patient with a history of IE 20 years ago** — high-risk, yes, but only if they have residual valvular damage. If they had a full recovery and no residual lesion, some guidelines (ESC) still consider previous IE a high-risk condition regardless of current valve state.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Infective endocarditis (IE) prophylaxis refers to the administration of antibiotics before certain invasive procedures to prevent bacteremia from adhering to damaged heart valves or prosthetic material and causing a vegetation. It is **not** about treating existing infections.

This topic matters because:
- IE is rare (3–10 per 100,000/year) but has high mortality (~20–30% in hospital, higher if prosthetic valve or staphylococcal).
- Prophylaxis guidelines have **narrowed** dramatically in the last two decades. Many clinicians (and patients) continue to use outdated regimens, especially for moderate-risk conditions like mitral valve prolapse, bicuspid aortic valve, or regurgitant murmurs without high-risk features.
- **Common pitfalls:** over-prescribing leads to antibiotic resistance, C. diff, anaphylaxis, and unnecessary cost. Under-prescribing in truly high-risk patients exposes them to preventable IE.
- Medico-legal: if a high-risk patient develops IE after a dental procedure where prophylaxis was omitted, it is a potential claim.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of IE development:**

- **Step 1: Endothelial injury.** Damaged or abnormal endocardium (from turbulent flow, prosthetic material, prior IE scarring) exposes collagen and tissue factor. This creates a **nonthrombogenic surface** that becomes a site for platelet and fibrin deposition — a sterile vegetation.
- **Step 2: Bacteremia.** Transient bacteremia occurs during daily activities (brushing teeth, chewing) but is **more intense** after dental procedures (especially extractions, scaling). The most common oral bacteria are *Streptococcus viridans* (alpha-hemolytic streptococci). Other organisms (*Staph aureus*, enterococci) are more common in healthcare-associated IE.
- **Step 3: Adherence and vegetation formation.** Bacteria adhere to the sterile platelet-fibrin matrix via surface adhesins (e.g., FimA in S. viridans). They multiply within the thrombus, protected from host immune cells. The vegetation enlarges, causing valve destruction, emboli, and immune phenomena.

**Why each key clinical sign happens (prophylaxis context):**

- **History of previous IE** — the healed valve often has fibrosis/deformity that predisposes to recurrent colonization. Mechanism: scarred endothelium is more thrombogenic.
- **Prosthetic valve** — artificial material (mechanical or bioprosthetic) lacks endothelial coverage and is highly thrombogenic. Bacteria adhere easily. Staph epidermidis is common here (coagulase-negative staph).
- **Cyanotic congenital heart disease (unrepaired)** — high-velocity shunts create turbulent jets that damage endothelium. Examples: tetralogy of Fallot, transposition.
- **Repaired congenital heart disease with prosthetic material in first 6 months** — incomplete endothelialization of the prosthetic graft or patch provides a raw surface for bacterial adhesion.

**Connecting to the pattern recognition clues:**

- The patient with an annuloplasty ring has a **prosthetic material** — even though it is a “repair” ring, it behaves biomechanically like a prosthetic valve regarding infection risk. Hence, high-risk.
- The patient with “heart murmur” but no high-risk features (e.g., functional murmur, mild aortic sclerosis) does **not** need prophylaxis because their endothelium is intact or only mildly abnormal — the risk-benefit is not worth it.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
The “cause” for giving prophylaxis is a **combination** of a high-risk patient *and* a procedure that produces significant bacteremia. We split into patient risk factors and procedure triggers.

#### Patient Risk Factors (High-Risk – need prophylaxis)

| Category | Specific condition | Stem clue |
|----------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Prosthetic valves** | Any mechanical or bioprosthetic valve, annuloplasty ring, homograft | "Had a valve replaced" or "mitral ring" |
| **Previous IE** | Prior episode, any valve | "History of infective endocarditis" |
| **Congenital heart disease (CHD)** | Unrepaired cyanotic CHD (e.g., tetralogy); repaired with prosthetic material (first 6 months); residual defects adjacent to prosthetic material | "Blue baby" or "shunt operation" |
| **Cardiac transplant** | With valvulopathy (usually tricuspid or mitral regurgitation) | "Transplant, with murmur on echo" |

#### Procedure Triggers (where prophylaxis is indicated in high-risk patients)

| Category | Examples | Stem clue |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Dental procedures** that manipulate gingiva or periapical region or perforate oral mucosa | Extractions, scaling/root planing, implant placement, reimplantation of avulsed teeth, subgingival restoration | "Dental extraction tomorrow" |
| **Non-dental** (only in high-risk — rarely needed) | Tonsillectomy, bronchoscopy with incision of respiratory mucosa (very high-risk patients only) | Not commonly encountered |

**Most common cause for prophylaxis:** A high-risk patient (especially with a prosthetic valve) scheduled for a dental extraction.

| **What does NOT trigger prophylaxis** (common mistakes) |
|----------------------------------------------------------|
| All GI/GU procedures (colonoscopy, cystoscopy, ERCP, etc.) — even in high-risk patients |
| Routine dental cleanings that do not involve gingival manipulation (e.g., polishing above gumline) |
| Injection of local anesthetic through non-infected skin (e.g., dental LA without abscess) |
| Shedding of primary teeth |

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
“Investigations” for prophylaxis is about **risk stratification** rather than a diagnostic test. You need to determine two things: (1) Is the patient high-risk? (2) Is the procedure high-risk?

**Step 1: Identify the patient’s cardiac status.**

| Investigation | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|---------------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Echocardiogram report** (TTE or TTE+TOE) | Valve morphology, presence of prosthetic material, previous vegetation | To confirm if patient meets high-risk criteria | Look for phrases: "prosthetic valve," "annuloplasty ring," "previous IE scar," "cyanotic CHD," "residual shunt". Patients with trivial mitral regurgitation or mild aortic sclerosis do NOT qualify. |
| **History & old notes** | Previous IE episodes, cardiac surgeries | To check for prior IE or valve repair | Clear documentation of "previous IE" is sufficient even if echo is now normal (ESC says high-risk). |
| **Dental assessment** | Need for extraction, presence of abscess, extent of gingival manipulation | To confirm procedure is high-risk | The dentist may say "simple cleaning" — if no gingival bleeding, no prophylaxis. |

**Step 2: Procedure classification.**

- **High-risk dental procedures:** Those involving bleeding from the gingiva or oral mucosa. If doubt, ask dentist.
- **No need for prophylaxis:** Local anesthetic only, removal of sutures, intraoral x-rays, placement of orthodontic brackets.

**What single finding confirms you must give prophylaxis?** A prosthetic heart valve (mechanical or bioprosthetic) or a documented previous IE — plus a dental extraction. That’s it. No other tests needed.

**What single finding rules out prophylaxis?** Any cardiac condition that is not in the high-risk list (e.g., mitral valve prolapse without regurgitation, bicuspid aortic valve without stenosis/regurgitation, atrial septal defect repaired without prosthetic material). If the patient has none of the high-risk features, you do not give antibiotics, regardless of the procedure.

**Diagnostic scoring system?** None — this is a risk classification, not a diagnostic score. Some guidelines (NICE) use a risk-averse approach: only advise prophylaxis for patients at *very high risk* as defined above. The US (AHA) and European (ESC) are similar but slightly broader (include some CHD repairs). For UK juniors, follow NICE guidelines (2015, updated 2022) which have narrowed the list to the four categories above.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour – before the procedure)**

1. **Confirm indication:** Ensure patient is high-risk (list above) AND procedure is high-risk (dental with gingival manipulation). If yes, proceed.
2. **Check allergy status:** If penicillin allergic → use alternative.
3. **Prescribe antibiotic:**
   - **First-line:** Amoxicillin 2 g PO (or 2 g IV if unable to swallow) given 30–60 minutes before procedure.
   - **If penicillin allergic:** Clindamycin 600 mg PO (or 600 mg IV) 30–60 minutes before.
   - **If IV needed and penicillin allergic:** Vancomycin 1 g IV over 1–2 hours (or teicoplanin 400 mg IV) – but this is rarely needed and usually reserved for inpatients.
4. **Document:** Write clearly in the drug chart: “For dental extraction tomorrow, give amoxicillin 2g PO 30-60 mins pre-procedure.”

**Why this timing and dose:** The antibiotic must be at peak serum concentration during the bacteremia. Amoxicillin 2g achieves bactericidal levels against viridans group streptococci for several hours.

**Ward (24–72 hours – if missed dose or post-procedure)**

- If the patient forgot to take the antibiotic, they can still take it up to 2 hours *after* the procedure (though efficacy is reduced). If more than 2 hours have passed, it’s too late — do not give a catch-up dose.
- If the patient develops a fever or signs of bacteremia (e.g., rigors, hypotension) within 48 hours of the procedure, treat as possible IE suspicion: send blood cultures, start empiric antibiotics (e.g., IV amoxicillin + gentamicin) if high-risk and unwell – involve cardiology.

**Long-term – what’s next?**

- **No ongoing need for repeat prophylaxis** unless further procedures.
- **Educate patient:** High-risk patients should maintain excellent dental hygiene and have regular dental checkups (every 6 months) to reduce spontaneous bacteremia.
- **Consider antibiotic prophylaxis for non-dental procedures?** Even in high-risk patients, GI/GU procedures no longer require it (NICE). Exception: if the procedure is on an infected skin/soft tissue focus (e.g., incision and drainage of an abscess in a high-risk patient) — then give antibiotics directed at the likely organism.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**

- ❌ **Do not give prophylaxis for low/moderate risk conditions** (e.g., mitral valve prolapse, bicuspid aortic valve, small VSD, regurgitant murmurs without prosthetic material). This is the most common mistake.
- ❌ **Do not give for colonoscopy** even if high-risk patient.
- ❌ **Do not use clindamycin routinely** if no penicillin allergy — it has higher C. diff risk and lower efficacy against viridans streptococci.
- ❌ **Do not give a dose more than 60 min before** — levels may fall off.
- ❌ **Do not repeat the dose if the patient vomits** after <30 min — consider IV if unable to retain.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
The “differential” for *prophylaxis* is really about distinguishing which patients need it vs. which do not. Four common presentations that cause confusion:

| Feature | High-risk patient (needs prophylaxis) | Patient with MVP without MR (no) | Patient with bicuspid AV without stenosis (no) | Patient with pacemaker (no) |
|---------|---------------------------------------|----------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **History** | Previous IE, prosthetic valve, cyanotic CHD, transplant valvulopathy | Asymptomatic; may have mid-systolic click | Often discovered incidentally on echo; may have aortic murmur but no symptoms | Usually after MI or conduction disease; no native valve issue |
| **Exam** | Mid-systolic murmur from prosthesis, or loud continuous murmur in CHD | No murmur, only click; no regurgitation | Ejection systolic murmur at right upper sternal edge; no signs of flow turbulence | Palpable box under skin; no cardiac murmur unless coexisting valve disease |
| **Echo findings** | Prosthetic valve, regurgitation/stenosis, vegetations (if previous IE), CHD anatomy | Normal LV; thin leaflets; minimal or no regurgitation | Bicuspid valve; no stenosis or regurgitation | Wires in right ventricle; no valve pathology |
| **Guidelines** | Prophylaxis YES for dental procedures with bleeding | Prophylaxis NO | Prophylaxis NO (unless severe stenosis? Still no per NICE) | Prophylaxis NO (not a valve lesion) |
| **Key distinguishing clue** | "Had a valve replacement" or "previous endocarditis" | "Click without murmur" | "Bicuspid valve, no gradient" | "Pacemaker for heart block" |

**Other mimics** – a patient with a ventriculoatrial shunt (used for hydrocephalus) – not a cardiac high-risk condition per se, but some clinicians mistakenly think so. Guidance: no prophylaxis for dental unless the patient has a concurrent high-risk cardiac condition.

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
If you fail to give prophylaxis in a truly high-risk patient undergoing a high-risk procedure, the consequence is **infective endocarditis** — a serious, preventable infection.

**Complication 1: Acute IE (especially if organism is *Staph aureus* or *Streptococcus* species)**

- **Presentation:** Fevers, rigors, night sweats, new or changed heart murmur, splinter hemorrhages, Osler’s nodes (painful), Janeway lesions (painless), Roth spots, hematuria, embolic stroke.
- **Key signs:** Consider IE in any high-risk patient with unexplained fever within 2 weeks of a dental procedure. Listen for a new regurgitant murmur.
- **What to do:** 
  - Blood cultures (two sets from different sites before antibiotics).
  - TTE/TOE to look for vegetation.
  - Urgent cardiology and microbiology input.
  - Empiric antibiotics: if IE suspected, start IV amoxicillin 2 g 4-hourly + gentamicin 1 mg/kg 8-hourly (or vancomycin if prosthetic) – discuss with microbiology.
- **Late presentation:** May present with heart failure (valve destruction), embolic stroke, or mycotic aneurysm.

**Complication 2: Prosthetic valve IE**

- **Presentation:** Same as above but more aggressive. Often caused by *Staph epidermidis* or *Viridans* group streptococci.
- **Signs:** Early dehiscence (paravalvular leak) on echo, new periprosthetic regurgitation, valve instability.
- **What to do:** Higher mortality; often requires surgery (valve replacement) in addition to prolonged IV antibiotics (6 weeks).

**What happens if untreated:**  
- Valve destruction → severe regurgitation → flash pulmonary edema, cardiogenic shock.  
- Embolism to brain → stroke, mycotic aneurysm, intracranial hemorrhage.  
- Embolism to kidney → renal abscess, hematuria.  
- Embolism to spleen → splenic abscess (left upper quadrant pain, fever).  
- Sepsis → multi-organ failure.

**Pattern-recognition for “missed prophylaxis” stems:**  
- A 75-year-old with a prosthetic aortic valve who had a tooth extracted 10 days ago now presents with fever and a new murmur. High index of suspicion: diagnosis is IE until proven otherwise. Do not delay blood cultures or echo.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic for high-risk conditions (UK NICE):**  
**P – Prosthetic valve** (any – mechanical, bioprosthetic, ring)  
**R – Previous IE** (any time)  
**O – (no O; we skip)**  
**P – (again)**  
**H – Heart transplant with valvulopathy**  
**Y – (not used)**  

Alternative: **“Prior IE, Prosthetic valve, Cyanotic CHD, Transplant valvulopathy”** – just memorize the four.

**Mnemonic for when to give prophylaxis:**  
**D – Dental extraction / scaling that bleeds**  
**O – Only in high-risk patients**  
**T – Thirty to 60 minutes before**  
**S – Single dose (no need for post-op)**

**5–7 Clinical Pearls (Pattern-recognition cues)**

1. **“Click and no murmur”** = mitral valve prolapse without regurgitation → NO prophylaxis.  
2. **“Prosthetic ring”** is still a prosthetic material – treat as high-risk.  
3. **“Previous IE”** is always high-risk, even if the valve is now normal on echo (risk of recurrence is higher).  
4. **“Dental abscess treated last month”** – no, the risk period is during the procedure when bacteremia occurs. Prophylaxis does not cover spontaneous bacteremia from ongoing infection – treat the abscess first.  
5. **“Colonoscopy”** – even in a high-risk patient, guidelines do not recommend prophylaxis. If the patient insists, explain that the risk of IE from GI procedures is negligible and antibiotics may cause harm.  
6. **“Allergic to penicillin”** – clindamycin 600 mg PO is second-line. It has lower efficacy (some resistance in viridans strep) and higher C. diff. Use amoxicillin if no true allergy (rash only is not always a contraindication – check).  
7. **“Dose missed”** – can be given up to 2 hours post-procedure, but not recommended if >2 hours; do not give a dose the next day.

**Common exam/ward mistakes**

- **The “murmur” reflex:** Any heart murmur does NOT mean prophylaxis. Always check the exact condition.
- **Assuming previous IE prophylaxis is lifelong:** It is not. Patients do not carry a “prophylaxis card” that covers all future procedures – it must be re-evaluated each time.
- **Confusing “rheumatic fever prophylaxis” (penicillin V daily) with IE prophylaxis:** They are different indications and durations.
- **Writing “stat” on a drug chart without a time:** The antibiotic must be given 30–60 min before; if not timed, it may be given too early or too late.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior / cardiologist / microbiologist**

- **Indeterminate risk:** You cannot find any records of the patient’s cardiac history (e.g., new patient, no echo report, patient unclear). **Call cardiology or the cardiac surgery team** to review the echo and confirm whether they are high-risk.
- **Penicillin allergy history unclear:** If the patient reports anaphylaxis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, or severe rash – do not use penicillin. If they only say “I think I’m allergic” but can’t recall symptoms – call microbiology for advice on desensitisation or safe alternative (e.g., cefazolin? but cephalosporins have cross-reactivity risk).
- **Missed prophylaxis in high-risk patient who now has a fever:** This is a potential IE. **Call the medical registrar** for urgent assessment. The patient may need admission, blood cultures, and echo.
- **Patient with prosthetic valve and any fever within 2 weeks of a dental procedure:** **Consider septic screen and cardiology referral** – this is a high-risk scenario for prosthetic valve IE, which is a surgical emergency if valve dehiscence occurs.

**Specific thresholds for escalation (

## Infective Endocarditis — Prophylaxis

**One-liner:** Antibiotics given before certain procedures to prevent life-threatening valve infection in the highest-risk patients only — misuse is common.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

You are on call for the ward. The nurse asks you to review a 65-year-old man before his dental extraction tomorrow. He has a history of “heart murmur.” His drug chart shows amoxicillin 2 g PO stat prescribed. You check his notes: he had a mitral valve repair with an annuloplasty ring 10 years ago. Two months ago, he was treated for a dental abscess with antibiotics. You need to decide if this prophylaxis is appropriate.

**Dissecting the vignette:**

- **“Heart murmur”** — not specific; you need to know the *type* of valve lesion. Some murmurs (e.g., mitral valve prolapse without regurgitation) are NOT an indication. Others (prosthetic valve, previous IE) are.
- **“Mitral valve repair with annuloplasty ring”** — this is a prosthetic material used in repair. Guidelines consider any prosthetic valve (including annuloplasty rings) as **high-risk** for IE. Prophylaxis is recommended.
- **“Dental extraction”** — a procedure that involves manipulation of gingiva or periapical region, so it triggers prophylaxis in high-risk patients.
- **“Amoxicillin 2 g PO stat”** — correct dose and timing (30–60 minutes before procedure). But you still need to confirm the patient is truly high-risk.
- **“Treated for dental abscess two months ago”** — prior dental infection is a risk factor for IE, but not an indication for prophylaxis unless the patient is already high-risk.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **The patient with a “normal” echocardiogram** — e.g., a bicuspid aortic valve without stenosis or regurgitation is considered moderate risk in some guidelines, but current NICE and ESC do **not** recommend prophylaxis even for bicuspid valves. Many junior doctors over-prescribe here.
- **The elderly patient with a prosthetic valve having a colonoscopy** — older guidelines (pre-2008) recommended prophylaxis for GI procedures. Current guidelines say **no** for GI/GU procedures, even in high-risk. But some patients or dentists may still request it.
- **The patient with a history of IE 20 years ago** — high-risk, yes, but only if they have residual valvular damage. If they had a full recovery and no residual lesion, some guidelines (ESC) still consider previous IE a high-risk condition regardless of current valve state.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Infective endocarditis (IE) prophylaxis refers to the administration of antibiotics before certain invasive procedures to prevent bacteremia from adhering to damaged heart valves or prosthetic material and causing a vegetation. It is **not** about treating existing infections.

This topic matters because:
- IE is rare (3–10 per 100,000/year) but has high mortality (~20–30% in hospital, higher if prosthetic valve or staphylococcal).
- Prophylaxis guidelines have **narrowed** dramatically in the last two decades. Many clinicians (and patients) continue to use outdated regimens, especially for moderate-risk conditions like mitral valve prolapse, bicuspid aortic valve, or regurgitant murmurs without high-risk features.
- **Common pitfalls:** over-prescribing leads to antibiotic resistance, C. diff, anaphylaxis, and unnecessary cost. Under-prescribing in truly high-risk patients exposes them to preventable IE.
- Medico-legal: if a high-risk patient develops IE after a dental procedure where prophylaxis was omitted, it is a potential claim.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of IE development:**

- **Step 1: Endothelial injury.** Damaged or abnormal endocardium (from turbulent flow, prosthetic material, prior IE scarring) exposes collagen and tissue factor. This creates a **nonthrombogenic surface** that becomes a site for platelet and fibrin deposition — a sterile vegetation.
- **Step 2: Bacteremia.** Transient bacteremia occurs during daily activities (brushing teeth, chewing) but is **more intense** after dental procedures (especially extractions, scaling). The most common oral bacteria are *Streptococcus viridans* (alpha-hemolytic streptococci). Other organisms (*Staph aureus*, enterococci) are more common in healthcare-associated IE.
- **Step 3: Adherence and vegetation formation.** Bacteria adhere to the sterile platelet-fibrin matrix via surface adhesins (e.g., FimA in S. viridans). They multiply within the thrombus, protected from host immune cells. The vegetation enlarges, causing valve destruction, emboli, and immune phenomena.

**Why each key clinical sign happens (prophylaxis context):**

- **History of previous IE** — the healed valve often has fibrosis/deformity that predisposes to recurrent colonization. Mechanism: scarred endothelium is more thrombogenic.
- **Prosthetic valve** — artificial material (mechanical or bioprosthetic) lacks endothelial coverage and is highly thrombogenic. Bacteria adhere easily. Staph epidermidis is common here (coagulase-negative staph).
- **Cyanotic congenital heart disease (unrepaired)** — high-velocity shunts create turbulent jets that damage endothelium. Examples: tetralogy of Fallot, transposition.
- **Repaired congenital heart disease with prosthetic material in first 6 months** — incomplete endothelialization of the prosthetic graft or patch provides a raw surface for bacterial adhesion.

**Connecting to the pattern recognition clues:**

- The patient with an annuloplasty ring has a **prosthetic material** — even though it is a “repair” ring, it behaves biomechanically like a prosthetic valve regarding infection risk. Hence, high-risk.
- The patient with “heart murmur” but no high-risk features (e.g., functional murmur, mild aortic sclerosis) does **not** need prophylaxis because their endothelium is intact or only mildly abnormal — the risk-benefit is not worth it.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

The “cause” for giving prophylaxis is a **combination** of a high-risk patient *and* a procedure that produces significant bacteremia. We split into patient risk factors and procedure triggers.

#### Patient Risk Factors (High-Risk – need prophylaxis)

| Category | Specific condition | Stem clue |
|----------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Prosthetic valves** | Any mechanical or bioprosthetic valve, annuloplasty ring, homograft | "Had a valve replaced" or "mitral ring" |
| **Previous IE** | Prior episode, any valve | "History of infective endocarditis" |
| **Congenital heart disease (CHD)** | Unrepaired cyanotic CHD (e.g., tetralogy); repaired with prosthetic material (first 6 months); residual defects adjacent to prosthetic material | "Blue baby" or "shunt operation" |
| **Cardiac transplant** | With valvulopathy (usually tricuspid or mitral regurgitation) | "Transplant, with murmur on echo" |

#### Procedure Triggers (where prophylaxis is indicated in high-risk patients)

| Category | Examples | Stem clue |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Dental procedures** that manipulate gingiva or periapical region or perforate oral mucosa | Extractions, scaling/root planing, implant placement, reimplantation of avulsed teeth, subgingival restoration | "Dental extraction tomorrow" |
| **Non-dental** (only in high-risk — rarely needed) | Tonsillectomy, bronchoscopy with incision of respiratory mucosa (very high-risk patients only) | Not commonly encountered |

**Most common cause for prophylaxis:** A high-risk patient (especially with a prosthetic valve) scheduled for a dental extraction.

| **What does NOT trigger prophylaxis** (common mistakes) |
|----------------------------------------------------------|
| All GI/GU procedures (colonoscopy, cystoscopy, ERCP, etc.) — even in high-risk patients |
| Routine dental cleanings that do not involve gingival manipulation (e.g., polishing above gumline) |
| Injection of local anesthetic through non-infected skin (e.g., dental LA without abscess) |
| Shedding of primary teeth |

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

“Investigations” for prophylaxis is about **risk stratification** rather than a diagnostic test. You need to determine two things: (1) Is the patient high-risk? (2) Is the procedure high-risk?

**Step 1: Identify the patient’s cardiac status.**

| Investigation | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|---------------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Echocardiogram report** (TTE or TTE+TOE) | Valve morphology, presence of prosthetic material, previous vegetation | To confirm if patient meets high-risk criteria | Look for phrases: "prosthetic valve," "annuloplasty ring," "previous IE scar," "cyanotic CHD," "residual shunt". Patients with trivial mitral regurgitation or mild aortic sclerosis do NOT qualify. |
| **History & old notes** | Previous IE episodes, cardiac surgeries | To check for prior IE or valve repair | Clear documentation of "previous IE" is sufficient even if echo is now normal (ESC says high-risk). |
| **Dental assessment** | Need for extraction, presence of abscess, extent of gingival manipulation | To confirm procedure is high-risk | The dentist may say "simple cleaning" — if no gingival bleeding, no prophylaxis. |

**Step 2: Procedure classification.**

- **High-risk dental procedures:** Those involving bleeding from the gingiva or oral mucosa. If doubt, ask dentist.
- **No need for prophylaxis:** Local anesthetic only, removal of sutures, intraoral x-rays, placement of orthodontic brackets.

**What single finding confirms you must give prophylaxis?** A prosthetic heart valve (mechanical or bioprosthetic) or a documented previous IE — plus a dental extraction. That’s it. No other tests needed.

**What single finding rules out prophylaxis?** Any cardiac condition that is not in the high-risk list (e.g., mitral valve prolapse without regurgitation, bicuspid aortic valve without stenosis/regurgitation, atrial septal defect repaired without prosthetic material). If the patient has none of the high-risk features, you do not give antibiotics, regardless of the procedure.

**Diagnostic scoring system?** None — this is a risk classification, not a diagnostic score. Some guidelines (NICE) use a risk-averse approach: only advise prophylaxis for patients at *very high risk* as defined above. The US (AHA) and European (ESC) are similar but slightly broader (include some CHD repairs). For UK juniors, follow NICE guidelines (2015, updated 2022) which have narrowed the list to the four categories above.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour – before the procedure)**

1. **Confirm indication:** Ensure patient is high-risk (list above) AND procedure is high-risk (dental with gingival manipulation). If yes, proceed.
2. **Check allergy status:** If penicillin allergic → use alternative.
3. **Prescribe antibiotic:**
   - **First-line:** Amoxicillin 2 g PO (or 2 g IV if unable to swallow) given 30–60 minutes before procedure.
   - **If penicillin allergic:** Clindamycin 600 mg PO (or 600 mg IV) 30–60 minutes before.
   - **If IV needed and penicillin allergic:** Vancomycin 1 g IV over 1–2 hours (or teicoplanin 400 mg IV) – but this is rarely needed and usually reserved for inpatients.
4. **Document:** Write clearly in the drug chart: “For dental extraction tomorrow, give amoxicillin 2g PO 30-60 mins pre-procedure.”

**Why this timing and dose:** The antibiotic must be at peak serum concentration during the bacteremia. Amoxicillin 2g achieves bactericidal levels against viridans group streptococci for several hours.

**Ward (24–72 hours – if missed dose or post-procedure)**

- If the patient forgot to take the antibiotic, they can still take it up to 2 hours *after* the procedure (though efficacy is reduced). If more than 2 hours have passed, it’s too late — do not give a catch-up dose.
- If the patient develops a fever or signs of bacteremia (e.g., rigors, hypotension) within 48 hours of the procedure, treat as possible IE suspicion: send blood cultures, start empiric antibiotics (e.g., IV amoxicillin + gentamicin) if high-risk and unwell – involve cardiology.

**Long-term – what’s next?**

- **No ongoing need for repeat prophylaxis** unless further procedures.
- **Educate patient:** High-risk patients should maintain excellent dental hygiene and have regular dental checkups (every 6 months) to reduce spontaneous bacteremia.
- **Consider antibiotic prophylaxis for non-dental procedures?** Even in high-risk patients, GI/GU procedures no longer require it (NICE). Exception: if the procedure is on an infected skin/soft tissue focus (e.g., incision and drainage of an abscess in a high-risk patient) — then give antibiotics directed at the likely organism.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**

- ❌ **Do not give prophylaxis for low/moderate risk conditions** (e.g., mitral valve prolapse, bicuspid aortic valve, small VSD, regurgitant murmurs without prosthetic material). This is the most common mistake.
- ❌ **Do not give for colonoscopy** even if high-risk patient.
- ❌ **Do not use clindamycin routinely** if no penicillin allergy — it has higher C. diff risk and lower efficacy against viridans streptococci.
- ❌ **Do not give a dose more than 60 min before** — levels may fall off.
- ❌ **Do not repeat the dose if the patient vomits** after <30 min — consider IV if unable to retain.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

The “differential” for *prophylaxis* is really about distinguishing which patients need it vs. which do not. Four common presentations that cause confusion:

| Feature | High-risk patient (needs prophylaxis) | Patient with MVP without MR (no) | Patient with bicuspid AV without stenosis (no) | Patient with pacemaker (no) |
|---------|---------------------------------------|----------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **History** | Previous IE, prosthetic valve, cyanotic CHD, transplant valvulopathy | Asymptomatic; may have mid-systolic click | Often discovered incidentally on echo; may have aortic murmur but no symptoms | Usually after MI or conduction disease; no native valve issue |
| **Exam** | Mid-systolic murmur from prosthesis, or loud continuous murmur in CHD | No murmur, only click; no regurgitation | Ejection systolic murmur at right upper sternal edge; no signs of flow turbulence | Palpable box under skin; no cardiac murmur unless coexisting valve disease |
| **Echo findings** | Prosthetic valve, regurgitation/stenosis, vegetations (if previous IE), CHD anatomy | Normal LV; thin leaflets; minimal or no regurgitation | Bicuspid valve; no stenosis or regurgitation | Wires in right ventricle; no valve pathology |
| **Guidelines** | Prophylaxis YES for dental procedures with bleeding | Prophylaxis NO | Prophylaxis NO (unless severe stenosis? Still no per NICE) | Prophylaxis NO (not a valve lesion) |
| **Key distinguishing clue** | "Had a valve replacement" or "previous endocarditis" | "Click without murmur" | "Bicuspid valve, no gradient" | "Pacemaker for heart block" |

**Other mimics** – a patient with a ventriculoatrial shunt (used for hydrocephalus) – not a cardiac high-risk condition per se, but some clinicians mistakenly think so. Guidance: no prophylaxis for dental unless the patient has a concurrent high-risk cardiac condition.

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

If you fail to give prophylaxis in a truly high-risk patient undergoing a high-risk procedure, the consequence is **infective endocarditis** — a serious, preventable infection.

**Complication 1: Acute IE (especially if organism is *Staph aureus* or *Streptococcus* species)**

- **Presentation:** Fevers, rigors, night sweats, new or changed heart murmur, splinter hemorrhages, Osler’s nodes (painful), Janeway lesions (painless), Roth spots, hematuria, embolic stroke.
- **Key signs:** Consider IE in any high-risk patient with unexplained fever within 2 weeks of a dental procedure. Listen for a new regurgitant murmur.
- **What to do:** 
  - Blood cultures (two sets from different sites before antibiotics).
  - TTE/TOE to look for vegetation.
  - Urgent cardiology and microbiology input.
  - Empiric antibiotics: if IE suspected, start IV amoxicillin 2 g 4-hourly + gentamicin 1 mg/kg 8-hourly (or vancomycin if prosthetic) – discuss with microbiology.
- **Late presentation:** May present with heart failure (valve destruction), embolic stroke, or mycotic aneurysm.

**Complication 2: Prosthetic valve IE**

- **Presentation:** Same as above but more aggressive. Often caused by *Staph epidermidis* or *Viridans* group streptococci.
- **Signs:** Early dehiscence (paravalvular leak) on echo, new periprosthetic regurgitation, valve instability.
- **What to do:** Higher mortality; often requires surgery (valve replacement) in addition to prolonged IV antibiotics (6 weeks).

**What happens if untreated:**  
- Valve destruction → severe regurgitation → flash pulmonary edema, cardiogenic shock.  
- Embolism to brain → stroke, mycotic aneurysm, intracranial hemorrhage.  
- Embolism to kidney → renal abscess, hematuria.  
- Embolism to spleen → splenic abscess (left upper quadrant pain, fever).  
- Sepsis → multi-organ failure.

**Pattern-recognition for “missed prophylaxis” stems:**  
- A 75-year-old with a prosthetic aortic valve who had a tooth extracted 10 days ago now presents with fever and a new murmur. High index of suspicion: diagnosis is IE until proven otherwise. Do not delay blood cultures or echo.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic for high-risk conditions (UK NICE):**  
**P – Prosthetic valve** (any – mechanical, bioprosthetic, ring)  
**R – Previous IE** (any time)  
**O – (no O; we skip)**  
**P – (again)**  
**H – Heart transplant with valvulopathy**  
**Y – (not used)**  

Alternative: **“Prior IE, Prosthetic valve, Cyanotic CHD, Transplant valvulopathy”** – just memorize the four.

**Mnemonic for when to give prophylaxis:**  
**D – Dental extraction / scaling that bleeds**  
**O – Only in high-risk patients**  
**T – Thirty to 60 minutes before**  
**S – Single dose (no need for post-op)**

**5–7 Clinical Pearls (Pattern-recognition cues)**

1. **“Click and no murmur”** = mitral valve prolapse without regurgitation → NO prophylaxis.  
2. **“Prosthetic ring”** is still a prosthetic material – treat as high-risk.  
3. **“Previous IE”** is always high-risk, even if the valve is now normal on echo (risk of recurrence is higher).  
4. **“Dental abscess treated last month”** – no, the risk period is during the procedure when bacteremia occurs. Prophylaxis does not cover spontaneous bacteremia from ongoing infection – treat the abscess first.  
5. **“Colonoscopy”** – even in a high-risk patient, guidelines do not recommend prophylaxis. If the patient insists, explain that the risk of IE from GI procedures is negligible and antibiotics may cause harm.  
6. **“Allergic to penicillin”** – clindamycin 600 mg PO is second-line. It has lower efficacy (some resistance in viridans strep) and higher C. diff. Use amoxicillin if no true allergy (rash only is not always a contraindication – check).  
7. **“Dose missed”** – can be given up to 2 hours post-procedure, but not recommended if >2 hours; do not give a dose the next day.

**Common exam/ward mistakes**

- **The “murmur” reflex:** Any heart murmur does NOT mean prophylaxis. Always check the exact condition.
- **Assuming previous IE prophylaxis is lifelong:** It is not. Patients do not carry a “prophylaxis card” that covers all future procedures – it must be re-evaluated each time.
- **Confusing “rheumatic fever prophylaxis” (penicillin V daily) with IE prophylaxis:** They are different indications and durations.
- **Writing “stat” on a drug chart without a time:** The antibiotic must be given 30–60 min before; if not timed, it may be given too early or too late.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call your senior / cardiologist / microbiologist**

- **Indeterminate risk:** You cannot find any records of the patient’s cardiac history (e.g., new patient, no echo report, patient unclear). **Call cardiology or the cardiac surgery team** to review the echo and confirm whether they are high-risk.
- **Penicillin allergy history unclear:** If the patient reports anaphylaxis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, or severe rash – do not use penicillin. If they only say “I think I’m allergic” but can’t recall symptoms – call microbiology for advice on desensitisation or safe alternative (e.g., cefazolin? but cephalosporins have cross-reactivity risk).
- **Missed prophylaxis in high-risk patient who now has a fever:** This is a potential IE. **Call the medical registrar** for urgent assessment. The patient may need admission, blood cultures, and echo.
- **Patient with prosthetic valve and any fever within 2 weeks of a dental procedure:** **Consider septic screen and cardiology referral** – this is a high-risk scenario for prosthetic valve IE, which is a surgical emergency if valve dehiscence occurs.
